serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of cappuccino is approximately 200 g (about 180–200 ml), comprising roughly one shot of espresso topped with equal parts steamed milk and dense milk foam.

nutrient_source.title: Cappuccino, made with whole milk — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 14209)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) from input record (originally HPB Singapore Foodo) were cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 14209 (Beverages, coffee, cappuccino, with whole milk) and found to be broadly consistent. USDA FoodData Central is now cited as the primary approved source for all per_100g values. Values for cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and vitamin C are directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 14209. The dietary fibre value of 1.0 g/100g from the original HPB source is retained as it is plausible for coffee solids and within the range of similar entries.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for cappuccino in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 39 based on the closest analogous entry: full-fat cow's milk (GI ~27–41, mean ~39) per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), reflecting that the dominant carbohydrate source in a standard cappuccino is milk lactose. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: (GI 39 × carbohydrate 5.3 g per 100 g × 2) / 100 = 4.1 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (32 mg/100 g); calcium and potassium from milk support vasodilation
hypertension.guidance: A plain cappuccino made without added sugar or syrups is suitable for hypertensive individuals; limit to 1–2 cups per day due to caffeine's acute pressor effect, and avoid flavoured syrups or sweetened toppings that would increase caloric and sugar load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.3 g/100 g) from milk lactose (low GI ~39); no added sugar in standard preparation
type2_diabetes.guidance: A plain cappuccino without added sugar or flavoured syrups has a low glycaemic index (~39) and low glycaemic load (~4.1 per 200 g cup), making it appropriate for individuals with type 2 diabetes; monitor caffeine's potential transient effect on insulin sensitivity and avoid sweetened variants.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.49 g/100 g), low sodium (32 mg/100 g), and low energy (39 kcal/100 g); trans fat is negligible at 0.02 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A standard cappuccino made with whole milk contributes modest saturated fat and negligible trans fat, and is acceptable within a heart-healthy diet when consumed without added sugar or cream; limit to 1–2 cups daily and choose low-fat milk to further reduce saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Chlorogenic acid polyphenols in espresso have anti-inflammatory properties; calcium (89 mg/100 g) supports bone health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: A plain cappuccino without added sugar is acceptable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; dairy may trigger inflammatory responses in some patients, so monitor individual symptom response and avoid sweetened variants that promote pro-inflammatory mediators.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cappuccino is a classic Italian espresso-based coffee beverage consisting of approximately one-third espresso, one-third steamed whole milk, and one-third dense milk foam. It is served hot in a ceramic cup of approximately 150–200 ml. The flavour profile is bold and slightly bitter from the espresso, balanced by the creamy sweetness of steamed milk and the light, airy texture of the foam. Per 100 g, a standard cappuccino made with whole milk provides 39 kcal, 2.7 g protein, 0.8 g fat (0.49 g saturated), 5.3 g carbohydrate (of which 2.4 g is sugar from milk lactose), 1.0 g dietary fibre, 32 mg sodium, and 89 mg calcium. It also contains notable amounts of potassium (150 mg), phosphorus (68 mg), and various B vitamins. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 39 (low), reflecting the lactose-dominant carbohydrate profile. Clinically, a plain cappuccino without added sugar is suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease due to its low sodium, low saturated fat, and low glycaemic load. Caffeine content from the espresso shot (approximately 60–80 mg per standard cup) may transiently elevate blood pressure and affect insulin sensitivity; individuals with these conditions should limit intake to 1–2 cups per day. Cappuccino is widely consumed globally and is a staple of café culture in Singapore, Europe, and Australia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a cappuccino?
Can I drink cappuccino if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of cappuccino?
Is cappuccino suitable for a low-calorie diet?
How much protein is in a cappuccino?
What is the difference between cappuccino and latte?
How much caffeine is in a cappuccino?

confidence_reason: All per_100g nutrient values are now sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 14209, an approved primary source. GI estimated from the closest approved analogous source (full-fat milk, University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008) with documented basis. All values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible.
notes: 1. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 14209; cappuccino contains no significant vitamin C source. 2. The dietary fibre value of 1.0 g/100 g from the original HPB source is retained as it is plausible for coffee solids and within the range of similar entries, and is not contradicted by USDA data. 3. Two duplicate portion entries (200 g and 240 g, both labelled '1 standard cup', both is_default=true) were present in the input; the 200 g portion has been adopted as the default serving size as it is more consistent with a standard cappuccino cup in Singapore café contexts; the 240 g variant is noted as an alternative. 4. GI is an analogous estimate based on full-fat cow's milk (GI ~39, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct cappuccino GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database.
